Computer Architecture

Memory

Electronics

Computer Science

Programming

6 min read
Just now

--

Part 2 of the How Computers Actually Work series
Missed Part 1? Before reading this chapter, I recommend starting with Part 1 — We Need to Talk About RAM. In the previous chapter, we discovered the real problem: Electricity doesn’t naturally remember anything.
Today, we’re going to solve that problem.

📚 How Computers Actually Work — Series

• ✅ Part 1 — We Need to Talk About RAM

• 👉 Part 2 — How Does a Computer Remember a Single Bit? (You’re reading)

• Part 3 — Why Does RAM Read an Entire Row?

• Part 4 — We Need to Talk About CPU Cache

• Part 5 — How Can a Computer Remember Without Power?

• Part 6 — How Can a Computer Remember Without a Disk?

Let’s Build the World’s Smallest Memory
Forget 16 GB of RAM.
Forget your laptop.
Forget servers with hundreds of gigabytes of memory.
Imagine you’re one of the engineers building the world’s first computer.
Your manager walks over and says,
“I don’t need gigabytes. I don’t even need one byte.”
“I only need one bit.”
Just this.
1That’s it.
How difficult could it be?
At first glance…
It sounds almost trivial.
It isn’t.

Attempt #1 — Use a Wire
Every computer uses electricity.
So our first idea seems obvious.
Let’s store electricity inside a wire.
Connect the battery.
Electricity flows.
Disconnect the battery.
Everything disappears.
The wire remembers nothing.
Maybe the wire is too short.
Let’s make it longer.
Still nothing.
Use thicker wires?
Still nothing.
Use copper instead of another metal?
Nothing changes.
We’ve just learned something important.
A wire can carry electricity.
It cannot store electricity.

🤔 Think Like an Engineer
When engineers fail, they usually don’t ask,
“What component should I use?”
Instead they ask,
“Have I solved a similar problem somewhere else?”
Let’s do exactly that.

How Do Humans Store Things?
Suppose I hand you a glass of water.
Where would you keep it?
Probably in a bucket.
Why not pour it onto the floor?
Because the floor isn’t designed to store water.
A bucket is.
That simple idea changes everything.
Maybe electricity also needs a bucket.

Meet the Electrical Bucket
Engineers already had one.
It’s called a capacitor.
Forget the textbook definition for now.
Think of it as one thing.
A tiny bucket for electrical charge.
Unlike a wire, a capacitor can temporarily hold electrical charge.
Finally…
We have something capable of remembering.
Or do we?

Can a Capacitor Store a Bit?
Let’s invent a simple rule.
If the capacitor contains enough charge…
1If it doesn’t…
0Notice something interesting.
The computer doesn’t care about every possible voltage.
It only needs two meaningful states.
Exactly like a light switch.
ON → 1
OFF → 0Computers love binary because the hardware becomes much simpler.

Looks Like We Solved It…
Let’s celebrate.
We wanted memory.
We built a tiny electrical bucket.
Mission accomplished.
Right?
Not quite.
I have another question.
How do we put charge into the capacitor?
How do we remove it?
And more importantly…
How do we stop electricity from flowing whenever it wants?
Imagine filling a bucket that has no tap.
Water would keep flowing forever.
We need a valve.

An Electrical Valve
For water, we use a tap.
For electricity, engineers use a MOSFET.
For this chapter, forget all the complicated electronics.
Think of it as nothing more than an electronic switch.
Switch OFFBit Line X CapacitorNothing flows.
Turn the switch on.
Switch ONBit Line ───── CapacitorCharge can now move freely.
Simple.
Exactly what we needed.

We Accidentally Built a DRAM Cell
Let’s combine our two inventions.

• A capacitor that stores charge.

• A MOSFET that controls when charge can move.
Congratulations.
You’ve just built the smallest memory cell used inside modern DRAM.
Engineers call it a 1T1C Cell.

• 1 Transistor

• 1 Capacitor
One bit.

Writing a One
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Suppose we want to store a 1.
The controller closes the switch.
The data wire is driven to a high voltage.
Charge flows into the capacitor.
The controller opens the switch again.
The capacitor is now isolated.
The stored charge remains trapped.

Writing a Zero
Now suppose we want to erase it.
The controller closes the switch again.
This time, the data wire is driven to zero volts.
The charge leaves the capacitor.
The switch opens.
Empty Capacitor -> 0Easy.
Or at least…
It looks easy.

Reading Creates a New Problem
Now the CPU asks,
“What value is stored?”
Simple.
Connect the capacitor back to the wire.
Right?
Something unexpected happens.
The moment we reconnect them…
At that moment, the capacitor and the Bit Line are no longer isolated.
They’re electrically connected.
Whenever two conductors with different voltages are connected together, they naturally move toward a common voltage by sharing charge.
This phenomenon is called charge sharing.Both containers end up sharing the water.
For example, if:

• The capacitor is at 1.2 V

• The Bit Line is precharged to 0.6 V
Capacitor and bit line ended up shared voltage around 0.62 V
The moment we read the memory…
We accidentally disturb it.

Wait… Did We Just Damage the Data?
Yes.
And that’s what makes DRAM fascinating.
Reading isn’t passive.
Reading changes the circuit.
At first glance, this sounds like terrible engineering.
Who would build memory that gets disturbed every time you read it?
Engineers did.
Because they had another clever trick.

Meet the Sense Amplifier
The voltage on the wire changes only slightly.
Far too little for the CPU to notice.
So engineers built a tiny detective.
Its only job is to answer one question.
Did the voltage become slightly higher?
Or slightly lower?
That circuit is called the Sense Amplifier.
Once it decides whether the answer was 0 or 1, it immediately rewrites the original value back into the capacitor.
Every DRAM read is secretly two operations.

• Read the value.

• Restore the value.
It happens so quickly that software never notices.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Fighting Physics
There’s one final problem.
Even if nobody reads the capacitor…
It slowly leaks charge.
Think of a bucket with a tiny hole.
Leave it alone long enough…
And it eventually becomes empty.
That’s exactly what happens inside DRAM.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

To prevent data loss, the memory controller periodically refreshes every row before the charge disappears.
RAM isn’t forgetting because it’s broken.
It’s forgetting because physics never stops.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

We’ve Solved One Bit
Today we built the smallest memory cell inside a computer.
Not with software.
Not with magic.
Just two tiny electronic components working together.
Modern RAM simply repeats this idea billions of times.
But that creates another question.
If every cell stores only one bit…
How does a computer read an entire byte?
Or an integer?
Surely it doesn’t visit billions of cells one at a time.
That’s exactly what we’ll discover in the next chapter.
